costs: SES-proxy data infrastructure (administrative data-linkage/register build); low-cost option (self-declared SES checklist) has minimal fiscal footprint, while the high-cost option (verified administrative register linkage, akin to Admin3-scale infrastructure) lacks a published fiscal range in current Hungarian costing studies.
Exam redesign and psychometric validation costs, one-off plus recurring revalidation, magnitude unpublished in Hungarian sources.
Enforcement and monitoring costs across state, church, and foundation maintainers, unbudgeted and structurally complicated by maintainer plurality.
content.equity_impact: Likely reduces SES-skew within remaining selective places, but middle-SES or rural pupils without qualifying SES-proxy status could face reduced access if quotas displace them from the residual non-quota places, and the scenario offers no specific safeguard or compensating mechanism for this displaced group; it also does nothing to address the ~40% of general schools (60% in villages) that send zero pupils to tracked programs regardless of admission-rule design.
expected_benefits: Reduced SES-skew in admitted cohorts at the 6/8-year gate, addressing the documented near-zero intake share of multiply-disadvantaged pupils.
Preserves the approximately 10% competency-gain advantage associated with 6/8-year tracks for admitted students, since the track structure itself is untouched.

Lower political disruption than structural reform, since it does not touch the statutory definition of secondary education or require felmenő-rendszer treatment of the whole system, though this should not be read as evidence the approach is free of reversal risk given the 2026 Fidesz culture-war framing of any selection-related change.
content.goal: Reduce family-capital-driven sorting at the point of entry into 6/8-year gimnázium tracks, without dismantling the track structure itself.

implementation_steps.action: Design SES-proxy criteria and draft legislative or decree text, incorporating felmenő-rendszer phase-in requirements and at least the ~1-year statutory lead time established by past Nkt. amendments.

Legislate uniform admission-equity rules applicable across maintainer types, with explicit negotiation provisions for church/foundation maintainer autonomy agreements; given documented litigation and consultation risks, this step is more realistically multi-year than a single legislative session.
Pilot the SES-proxy admission mechanism and coaching-resistant exam format in a subset of regions/schools before national rollout, monitoring for circumvention.
National rollout with felmenő-rendszer cohort protections and published annual monitoring of admitted-cohort SES composition.

mechanisms: SES-aware quotas or proxy indicators reserve a share of places for disadvantaged applicants, directly countering the documented ~46% vs ~22% rejection-rate gap between low- and high-poverty-share classes.

A less coaching-sensitive exam format would reduce the weight of family test-prep resources in admission outcomes, though no Hungarian evidence confirms any specific alternative format's effectiveness.

Uniform score-threshold rules across maintainers would reduce administratively inconsistent, tightening cutoffs (e.g., 70/100 points at some institutions) that currently vary by maintainer discretion.
risks: Implementation by ministerial decree rather than cardinal law leaves the mechanism exposed to reversal by a subsequent government within a single electoral cycle; the scenario does not propose a specific entrenchment design (e.g., cardinal-law status, cross-party pact, or sunset-clause safeguard) to mitigate this.
Fidesz's demonstrated framing of any selection-related change as ideologically totalitarian creates reversal risk even for a narrower admissions-only intervention, not only for structural abolition.

Church and foundation maintainer coalition constraints (Tisza's 12-point program commitment to preserve prior operating conditions) may block uniform enforcement, creating a two-tier compliance landscape.
content.title: Admissions and intake-equity regulation (keep structure, fix the gate)
